skill.name: test-case-generator
skill.description: Generate structured functional test cases from text, images, PDFs, and DOCX requirements. The skill follows a 15-field format and creates Excel-ready QA output.
skill.summary: Turn product requirements into structured QA test cases with positive, negative, boundary, and security scenarios.
skill.author: Devliang24

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many high and medium findings, but most are false positives caused by Markdown inline code, fenced examples, Chinese text entropy, and a benign Claude.ai documentation link. The skill has a real medium-risk behavior because it instructs Python code execution and writes an Excel file using a requirement-derived filename, so publication should include a warning about filesystem writes and filename handling.

medium_findings.title: Python Code Execution Required for Excel Generation
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill instructs use of Python and openpyxl to create a workbook. This is legitimate for Excel output, but it requires code execution and should run in a sandboxed environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files explicitly state that code execution is needed and provide Python workbook creation logic. The behavior is intentional and useful, but it expands the execution surface.

medium_findings.title: Requirement-Derived Filename Used for Workbook Save
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The sample code builds the Excel filename from requirement_name and saves it. If requirement_name is taken from user content without sanitization, path separators or unsafe names could affect where the file is written.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The save path is clearly derived from a variable, but the file does not define how requirement_name is populated. The risk is plausible and should be mitigated by filename sanitization.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The external command detections are caused by Markdown inline code, code fences, examples, and formatting notes. No evidence found of Ruby backtick execution or shell command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed locations show Markdown formatting and examples rather than executable shell syntax. The files contain instructions and documentation, not runnable Ruby code.

low_findings.title: Claude.ai Link Is Benign Documentation
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The only network finding is a hardcoded Claude.ai URL used in installation instructions. No evidence found that the skill makes runtime network requests or sends user data externally.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears in a numbered setup step for opening Claude.ai. It is not part of executable code or a data transfer flow.

low_findings.title: Entropy and Weak Crypto Alerts Are Text False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The high-entropy and weak-cryptography alerts map to Chinese prose, Markdown headings, and metadata. No evidence found of encoded payloads, encrypted blobs, or cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are readable Markdown and skill metadata. Manual review found no binary content, encoded payload, or weak hash use.

dangerous_patterns.title: Workbook Save Uses Unsanitized Output Name
dangerous_patterns.description: The code example saves an xlsx file with a name built from requirement_name. Implementations should strip path separators, control characters, and reserved filenames before saving.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filename construction is explicit, but the source and validation of requirement_name are not shown. The concern is bounded to filesystem hygiene.

content.value_statement: Product teams often need consistent, executable test cases from changing requirements. This skill turns text, PDFs, documents, and images into structured Excel-ready QA cases.
actual_capabilities: Extracts functional points, workflows, inputs, outputs, business rules, and edge cases from requirement documents.
Generates test cases using a fixed 15-field structure for consistent QA review.
Covers positive flows, negative scenarios, boundary values, security checks, compatibility checks, and usability checks.
Creates Excel-ready output with headers, wrapping, borders, alignment, and adjusted column widths.
Asks clarification questions when requirements lack verifiable details.
Provides a summary with total case counts and coverage by function.
limitations: Generated cases depend on the completeness and accuracy of the provided requirements.
The skill does not execute the generated test cases against an application.
Ambiguous rules may require user assumptions or clarification before output is reliable.
Excel generation requires a code execution environment with Python and openpyxl.

use_cases.title: Create Regression Suites from Product Requirements
use_cases.description: Convert new or updated PRDs into structured functional, boundary, and negative test cases for release testing.

use_cases.title: Review Requirement Test Coverage
use_cases.description: Generate draft cases that expose missing error states, validation rules, permissions, and edge conditions.

use_cases.title: Prepare Importable Test Case Spreadsheets
use_cases.description: Produce Excel-ready test case files that can be adapted for test management systems.

prompt_templates.title: Generate Basic Test Cases
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request with a short requirement.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate test cases from this requirement. Use the standard 15 fields and include positive, negative, and boundary scenarios.

prompt_templates.title: Create Excel-Ready Cases
prompt_templates.scenario: Standard request for a complete spreadsheet output.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the attached requirement document and create an Excel-ready test case set. Keep actual results empty and include a coverage summary.

prompt_templates.title: Focus on Security and Permissions
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for access control coverage.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate test cases for this feature with extra focus on authentication, authorization, sensitive data handling, and invalid access attempts.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Coverage Gaps Before Generation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for unclear or incomplete requirements.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this requirement for missing testable details first. Ask up to five clarification questions, then generate cases with assumptions listed if details remain unknown.

output_examples.input: A login requirement with username rules, password rules, failed login limits, and remember-me behavior.
output: A structured Excel-ready case set for successful login, missing fields, invalid credentials, username boundaries, lockout behavior, and password masking.
A summary showing total cases, positive cases, negative cases, priority distribution, and covered login functions.

output_examples.input: A file upload requirement with allowed extensions, maximum size, filename length, progress display, and error messages.
output: A test set covering successful uploads, file type validation, size boundaries, filename length boundaries, upload progress, and failure messages.
Each case includes preconditions, numbered steps, concrete test data, expected results, status, priority, and creator fields.

best_practices: Provide complete business rules, validation limits, role permissions, status transitions, and expected error messages.
Ask for assumptions to be listed when requirements are incomplete or time is limited.
Review generated cases before importing them into a test management system.
anti_patterns: Do not provide only a feature name without rules, states, inputs, or expected behavior.
Do not treat generated cases as executed test results.
Do not use unsanitized requirement names as filesystem paths when generating Excel files.

faq.question: What inputs can this skill use?
faq.answer: It can work from pasted text, images, PDFs, DOCX files, and other requirement documents available to the assistant.

faq.question: What format does it generate?
faq.answer: It generates Excel-ready test cases using a fixed 15-field structure defined by the skill.

faq.question: Does it include negative and boundary tests?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow explicitly plans positive flows, boundary values, exception paths, and relevant security checks.

faq.question: Can it ask clarification questions?
faq.answer: Yes. It should ask up to five questions when missing details affect executable or verifiable test cases.

faq.question: Does it run tests against my product?
faq.answer: No. It creates test case documentation and spreadsheets. It does not execute those tests.

faq.question: What security caution applies?
faq.answer: Excel generation uses code execution and writes a file. Run it in a sandbox and sanitize generated filenames.
